name: John Kirby
death: Middletown, Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America
facts: Event: Will / 6 April 1677: He made his Will
Event: Inventory of Estate / 27 April 1677
Event: Find-A-Grave / 27 April 1677: Memorial #194683715

memory:
John Kirby Will from:
 
THE HISTORY OF
ANCIENT WETHERSFIELD
CONNECTICUT

Page 473-475

KIRBY, JOHN. Furnished by Rev. M. E. DWIGHT, of Plainsfield, N. J. Of
Rowington in Warwickshire, Eng., came in the Hopewell, Capt. Babb, master,
in 1635, ae. twelve years. He was at Plymouth, Mass., in 1643, and at Hartford, Ct., before April, 1645. In 1647 he was a resident of Weth., Ct., for in
W. T. Y. I. 3, of that year, recorded his "ear-marks" for cattle. Three of his
children were born in Weth., and their births are recorded in the Town Records.
Some time between the birth of his twin children John and Eunice, Dec. 18,
1651, and Jan., 1654, he became a resident of Middletown, Ct.; his ho-std. was
situated on what is now Pleasant St., in extreme western part of (present)
Cromwell, in the bend of the Mattabesset, (present Little) River where the
stream turns S. E.—and the bridge just above the bend is still called Kirby's
Bridge. Betw. it is a small but picturesquely wooded island. The lib., the
foundations of which are still visible, stood on higher ground, sloping gradually
to the river. He died in April, 1677 possessed of a good estate, the inventory of
which taken April 27, 1677, (and which is in the probate volumne recovered by
Dr. Hoadly in N. Y., and now in See. State's office, Htfd.), showed a valuation
of £551-5-10. He had, ace to writer in Htfd. Times, no less than 9 Bibles.
His wife Elizabeth was the neice of Sarah (Hinds) Chaplin, wife of Mr.
Clement Chaplin, of Weth., Ct.

name: Francis Andrews
death: Fairfield, Fairfield, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America
facts: Event: Immigrated / 1639 / Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America
Occupation: Farmer
memory:
Francis Andrews probably immigrated to New England in the 1630's. He was in Hartford in 1639 and an early member of the First Church. He married Anna Smith, daughter of John Smith, in Hartford. He purchased the north part of Richard Butler's lot on the corner of Elm St. & Trinity St. His name is on the Founder's Monument in the Ancient Burying Ground at Hartford.
Around 1648, he removed to Fairfield where he lived until his death. His will written June 6, 1662 and proved March 5, 1662/3 names sons: John, Thomas, & Jeremiah, and daughters: Elizabeth, Mary, Hester, Rebecca & Ruth.
(from "Families of Early Hartford, Conn.")

memory:
According to the membership application to the Texas Society of the Sons of the American Revolution by James Robert Ingram:
Captain Thomas Jones served as captain of company recruited on Tiger River in 96th Dist. Water's Regiment, S.C. Militia. 
He was killed in skirmish with the British and Tories on 12 January 1781 at Clark's Ford, The Enoree River and was buried on the banks of said river near Clark's Ford.

memory:
"Bramlett road is the first road of importance mentioned in Bedford County Records. This was a east to west thoroughfare passing thru New London and what was later the town of LIBERTY and on to Botetourt County line. It was probably the same route as to the following Lynchburg and Salem turnpike built in the early 1830's and practically the same Bedford to Roanoke state highway 460 of today. It is believed that William Bramlett Sr. was part of building that road."

SOURCE:
Parker's History of Bedford County, Virginia
By: Lula Eastman Jeter Parker Hamilton Press September 1 1988

name: Augustine Herman
death: Cecil, Maryland, United States
facts: Residence: 24 May 1661 / Baltimore, Maryland, British Colonial America

Occupation: cartographer
Fact: Occupation: Cartographer
Residence: United States
memory:
Col. Augustine Herman, Lord of Bohemia Manor, Cecil County, Maryland was a great man of the region with vast estates in both New Castle County, Delaware, and Cecil County, Maryland ("Sidelights on Maryland History and Historical and Biographical Society of Delaware"). He named New Castle, Delaware. He lived in baronial splendor, perhaps unequalled any where else in this country. He had a deer park and rode about the countryside in a coach with liveried footmen. He spoke 6 languages in addition to being an artist, surveyor, map maker, diplomat, Colonel of Militia, writer of Indian Treaties, pioneer in the Virginia tobacco trade and was Presiding Justice of the Cecil County Court.
 He was born in Prague, Bohemia (now Czechoslovakia) about 1605. He was the son of Augustine Ephraim Herman, a wealthy merchant of Prague and Beatrice. She was the daughter of Caspar Von Ridel of a Patrician Bohemian Family. 
 It was said that Augustine's father was outlawed for signing a Protestant memorial to the Emperor of Germany in 1618 at the beginning of the Thirty Years War and that he probably died in battle. His widow and Augustine later came to New Amsterdam. Augustine was at the Battle of Lutzen under Wallenstein on March 11, 1632 where he felled the Swedish King - but the victory went to the Swedes. Augustine sailed for America in 1633 in the employ of the Dutch West India Company. In New Amsterdam he became a prosperous merchant, banker, lawyer and owner of a frigate engaged in privateer

memory:
Major Thomas Brooke was born at Battle, England in 1638 and arrived with his father in Maryland in 1650. He was commissioned Major in 1660. He represented Calvert County in the Provincial Assembly from 1663 to 1666 and was High Sheriff of the County from 1666 to 1667. He was presiding Justice of County Court in 1667. He married Eleanor Hatton who was the daughter of Richard and Margaret Hatton. She was born in 1642 and came to Maryland with her widowed mother and family in 1649. 

Col. Thomas Brooke 1659 first son

Robert Brooke 1663-1762

Clement Brooke 1676-1737

Mary Brooke (She married first Capt. John Bowling who died in 1693 and then married her second husband, Benjamin Hall of Prince George's County, Maryland, and he died in 1721. Third husband was Henry Witham.)

Eleanor Brooke (She married her first husband, Philip Darnall who died in 1705, he was the son of her step-father, Col. Henry Darnall; her second marriage was to William Digges.

memory:
Web Page (Link to the Record)
http://genealogical-gleanings.com/Early%20Virginia.htm
Where The Record Is Found (Citation)
Notes, http://genealogical-gleanings.com/Early%20Virginia.htm.
Describe The Record (Notes)
"Thomas Bastrop was born in 1635 in England. He probably died in King William, VA. He had a daughter, Ann, who married James/William Mackgahye below.

James/William Mackgahye was born in 1618 in Scotland. The surname was originally MacGregor but as the MacGregor Clann was outlawed in Scotland, James changed the name to Mackgayhe in order to avoid confrontation with British soldiers. James married Anne Bastrop who was born in Kent, England in 1664. James settled in King William Co., VA and had children: Thomas and William.

Thomas MackGehee was born in 1659 in LochKarine, Scotland, He married unknown. Thomas died about July 1727 in King William, VA. Thomas and Ann had the following children: Dinah, Abraham, Jacob, Samuel, Sarah, Anna, Mary and William. Thomas owned land in New Kent as early in 1689 in St.Peter's Parish. In 1702 Thomas petitioned for 256 acres in Pamunkey Neck in King William County. This patent was for transporting six persons into the Colony. In 1703 Thomas purchased 110 acres from William Glover in King William County. This land was further up river at Harrison Creek. The Quit Rent Rolls of 1704 for King William list Thomas MackGehee with 250 acres. Thomas's will was dated 27 July 1727 in St.John's Parish of King William County. He mentions son, Wil
